source_metadata.title_raw: Steven F. Nord - A-82-038 - March 9, 1982 - Merced

content.full_text:
A.G.I. U2G

March 9, 1962

Steven F. Nord
City Attorney's Office
561 W. 18th Street
Merced, CA 95340

Dear Steve:

This letter is sent in response to your telephone call and our phone discussions of February 18 and 22, 1962.

We discussed a potential conflict of interest situation involving two city council members and a zoning decision. I concluded, based on the facts you gave me, that it was a conflict of interest for these two officials to participate in a decision to re-zone approximately 1¼ lots in the vicinity of Mercy Hospital.

You then asked me whether it would be possible to divide the zoning decision into two ordinances, one dealing with the lot owned by Mercy Hospital which is the source of the conflict, and the other dealing with the remaining lots. In other words, would the officials have a conflict of interest as to both decisions or only as to the decision concerning the hospital's lot.

The officials must disqualify themselves as to both decisions if the result on one decision will effectively determine the result of the other decision. This would be true, for example, if the same policies and interests are at stake in both decisions. They must also disqualify themselves on both decisions if the decision is of the type that cannot be legally divided. For example, if there were different results on the two ordinances, a legal challenge could successfully compel the city council to arrive at a consistent

Senator P. Hord
March 9, 1962
Page Two

result. On the other hand, if the policies and equities
involved in the two decisions differ significantly, dividing
the decision into two separate ordinances would be an appropriate
manner to deal with the conflict of interest.

We did not have enough facts to apply the above tests
to your situation. Please feel free to contact me if you
would like to discuss this further.

Very truly yours,


Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:lm

embedding.qa_text: March 9, 1982 Steven F. Nord City Attorney's Office 561 W. 18th Street Merced, CA 95340 Dear Steve: This letter is sent in response to your telephone call and our phone discussions of February 18 and 22, 1982. We discussed a potential conflict of interest situation involving two city council members and a zoning decision. I concluded, based on the facts you gave me, that it was a conflict of interest for these two officials to participate in a decision to re-zone approximately 15 lots in the vicinity of Mercy Hospital. You then asked me whether it would be possible to divide the zoning decision into two ordinances, one dealing with the lot owned by Mercy Hospital which is the source of the conflict, and the other dealing with the remaining lots. In other words, would the officials have a conflict of interest as to both decisions or only as to the decision concerning the hospital's lot. The officials must disqualify themselves as to both decisions if the result on one decision will effectively determine the result of the other decision. This would be true, for example, if the same policies and interests are at stake in both decisions. They must also disqualify themselves on both decisions if the decision is of the type that cannot be legally divided. For example, if there were different results on the two ordinances, a legal challenge could successfully compel the city council to arrive at a consistent result. On the other hand, if the policies and equities involved in the two decisions differ significantly, dividing the decision into two separate ordinances would be an appropriate manner to deal with the conflicts of interest. We did not have enough facts to apply the above tests to your situation. Please feel free to contact me if you would like to discuss this further. Very truly yours, Diane Maera Fishburn Counsel Legal Division DMF:lm
